section.title: The Sabbath Year and the Jubilee
section.sectionRationale: This chapter extends the Sabbath principle from days to years, establishing the sabbatical year of rest for the land and, climactically, the fiftieth year Jubilee, when debts are released, land returns to its original families, and enslaved Israelites go free, grounded in the truth that the land belongs to the LORD.
section.thesis: God builds rest and restoration into the very structure of time, sabbatical years releasing the land and the Jubilee year releasing people and property, because the land is His and Israel are His redeemed servants, never permanently bound to poverty or slavery.
section.frameNote: Four movements: the Sabbath year of rest for the land; the fiftieth year Jubilee, proclaiming liberty and the return of property; laws of fair dealing in land sales and redemption tied to the Jubilee; and laws protecting the poor and enslaved countryman, since Israel are servants of the LORD alone.

verses.text: Then the LORD said to Moses on Mount Sinai,
“Speak to the Israelites and say to them: When you enter the land that I am giving you, the land itself must observe a Sabbath to the LORD.
For six years you may sow your field and prune your vineyard and gather its crops.
But in the seventh year there shall be a Sabbath of complete rest for the land, a Sabbath to the LORD. You are not to sow your field or prune your vineyard.
You are not to reap the aftergrowth of your harvest or gather the grapes of your untended vines. The land must have a year of complete rest.
Whatever the land yields during the Sabbath year shall be food for you, for yourself, your manservant and maidservant, the hired hand or foreigner who stays with you,
and for your livestock and the wild animals in your land. All its growth may serve as food.

media.alt: A ram's horn sounding across a landscape
media.caption: The horn sounds throughout the land, proclaiming liberty in the Year of Jubilee.

verses.text: And you shall count off seven Sabbaths of years, seven times seven years, so that the seven Sabbaths of years amount to forty-nine years.
Then you are to sound the horn far and wide on the tenth day of the seventh month, the Day of Atonement. You shall sound it throughout your land.

So you are to consecrate the fiftieth year and proclaim {{deror|liberty}} in the land for all its inhabitants. It shall be your {{yovel|Jubilee}}, when each of you is to return to his property and to his clan.
The fiftieth year will be a Jubilee for you; you are not to sow the land or reap its aftergrowth or harvest the untended vines.
For it is a Jubilee; it shall be holy to you. You may eat only the crops taken directly from the field.
In this Year of Jubilee, each of you shall return to his own property.

units.label: Fair dealing between neighbors
units.note: priced according to years until Jubilee

verses.text: If you make a sale to your neighbor or a purchase from him, you must not take advantage of each other.
You are to buy from your neighbor according to the number of years since the last Jubilee; he is to sell to you according to the number of harvest years remaining.
You shall increase the price in proportion to a greater number of years, or decrease it in proportion to a lesser number of years; for he is selling you a given number of harvests.
Do not take advantage of each other, but fear your God; for I am the LORD your God.
You are to keep My statutes and carefully observe My judgments, so that you may dwell securely in the land.
Then the land will yield its fruit, so that you can eat your fill and dwell in safety in the land.
Now you may wonder, ‘What will we eat in the seventh year if we do not sow or gather our produce?’
But I will send My blessing upon you in the sixth year, so that the land will yield a crop sufficient for three years.
While you are sowing in the eighth year, you will be eating from the previous harvest, until the ninth year’s harvest comes in.

units.label: The land is Mine, and redemption
units.note: a kinsman may buy it back

verses.text: The {{aretz|land}} must not be sold permanently, because it is Mine, and you are but foreigners and residents with Me.
Thus for every piece of property you possess, you must provide for the {{geullah|redemption}} of the land.
If your brother becomes impoverished and sells some of his property, his nearest of kin may come and redeem what his brother has sold.
Or if a man has no one to redeem it for him, but he prospers and acquires enough to redeem his land,
he shall calculate the years since its sale, repay the balance to the man to whom he sold it, and return to his property.

But if he cannot obtain enough to repay him, what he sold will remain in possession of the buyer until the Year of Jubilee. In the Jubilee, however, it is to be released, so that he may return to his property.

units.label: Houses in the city, and Levite cities

verses.text: If a man sells a house in a walled city, he retains his right of redemption until a full year after its sale; during that year it may be redeemed.
If it is not redeemed by the end of a full year, then the house in the walled city is permanently transferred to its buyer and his descendants. It is not to be released in the Jubilee.
But houses in villages with no walls around them are to be considered as open fields. They may be redeemed, and they shall be released in the Jubilee.
As for the cities of the Levites, the Levites always have the right to redeem their houses in the cities they possess.

So whatever belongs to the Levites may be redeemed, a house sold in a city they possess, and must be released in the Jubilee, because the houses in the cities of the Levites are their possession among the Israelites.
But the open pastureland around their cities may not be sold, for this is their permanent possession.
Now if your countryman becomes destitute and cannot support himself among you, then you are to help him as you would a foreigner or stranger, so that he can continue to live among you.
Do not take any interest or profit from him, but fear your God, that your countryman may live among you.
You must not lend him your silver at interest or sell him your food for profit.
I am the LORD your God, who brought you out of the land of Egypt to give you the land of Canaan and to be your God.

units.label: An Israelite who sells himself
units.note: a hired worker, not a slave

verses.text: If a countryman among you becomes destitute and sells himself to you, then you must not force him into slave labor.
Let him stay with you as a hired worker or temporary resident; he is to work for you until the Year of Jubilee.
Then he and his children are to be released, and he may return to his clan and to the property of his fathers.
Because the Israelites are My servants, whom I brought out of the land of Egypt, they are not to be sold as slaves.
You are not to rule over them harshly, but you shall fear your God.
Your menservants and maidservants shall come from the nations around you, from whom you may purchase them.
You may also purchase them from the foreigners residing among you or their clans living among you who are born in your land. These may become your property.
You may leave them to your sons after you to inherit as property; you can make them slaves for life. But as for your brothers, the Israelites, no man may rule harshly over his brother.

units.label: Redemption from a foreign master
units.note: released, at the latest, in the Jubilee

verses.text: If a foreigner residing among you prospers, but your countryman dwelling near him becomes destitute and sells himself to the foreigner or to a member of his clan,
he retains the right of redemption after he has sold himself. One of his brothers may redeem him:
either his uncle or cousin or any close relative from his clan may redeem him. Or if he prospers, he may redeem himself.

He and his purchaser will then count the time from the year he sold himself up to the Year of Jubilee. The price of his sale will be determined by the number of years, based on the daily wages of a hired hand.
If many years remain, he must pay for his redemption in proportion to his purchase price.
If only a few years remain until the Year of Jubilee, he is to calculate and pay his redemption according to his remaining years.
He shall be treated like a man hired from year to year, but a foreign owner must not rule over him harshly in your sight.
Even if he is not redeemed in any of these ways, he and his children shall be released in the Year of Jubilee.
For the Israelites are My servants. They are My servants, whom I brought out of the land of Egypt. I am the LORD your God.

deror.gloss: Liberty, release, or free flow. This exact word is echoed by Isaiah, proclaim liberty to the captives, Isaiah 61:1, which Jesus reads aloud in Luke 4 and declares fulfilled in Himself, tying the Jubilee directly to His own announced mission.

yovel.gloss: Jubilee, likely from the ram's horn, yovel, sounded to announce it. Every fiftieth year, land returned to its original family and enslaved Israelites went free, a structural, repeated reset against permanent poverty and bondage.

geullah.gloss: Redemption, the act of a near relative buying back land or a person from poverty or servitude. This kinsman redeemer role, later embodied by Boaz for Ruth and Naomi, becomes a central biblical picture fulfilled in Christ's redemption of His people.

aretz.gloss: Land, earth, or territory. Verse 23's declaration, the land is Mine, relativizes every human claim to permanent ownership, grounding Israel's entire economic system in the reality that they are tenants and stewards, not ultimate possessors, of what God gives.

basic.context: This chapter establishes two rhythms of rest: every seventh year the land itself rests, and every forty-ninth year's Day of Atonement, a Jubilee is proclaimed in which debts are released, land returns to its original family, and enslaved Israelites go free.
basic.hermeneutics: This is specific old covenant economic law tied to Israel's unique tribal land inheritance in Canaan; it is not a directly transferable blueprint for modern economic policy. Its enduring vision, that poverty and bondage are not to be permanent, finds its complete fulfillment when Jesus announces true liberty in Luke 4.
basic.originalLanguages: Deror, liberty, is the very word Isaiah later uses of a coming proclamation of release, which Jesus reads aloud and declares fulfilled in Himself. Yovel, Jubilee, likely from the ram's horn sounded to announce it, names this fiftieth year reset. Ge'ullah, redemption, describes a kinsman buying back what poverty had cost his relative, grounded in the declaration that the aretz, land, ultimately belongs to God alone.
basic.typology: The Jubilee's proclamation of liberty is fulfilled when Jesus reads Isaiah's Jubilee language in the Nazareth synagogue and declares it fulfilled that very day in Himself, Luke 4:18-21. The kinsman redeemer who buys back a relative's lost land or freedom anticipates Christ, who became one of us to redeem us from slavery to sin.
basic.christ: Luke 4:18-21 records Jesus reading Isaiah's words, the Spirit of the Lord is upon Me, to proclaim liberty to the captives, in the Nazareth synagogue, then declaring, today this Scripture is fulfilled in your hearing, directly claiming the Jubilee's ancient promise as His own present mission.
basic.god: God insists the land is His, relativizing every human claim to permanent wealth or power; He builds structural, repeated points of release into Israel's economic life so that poverty, debt, and slavery are never treated as permanent, reflecting His own character as Redeemer.
basic.crossReferences: Luke 4:18-21 records Jesus announcing Jubilee fulfillment in Himself. Isaiah 61:1-2 supplies the source text He reads. Galatians 4:4-5 says God sent His Son to redeem those under the law. Ruth 4 shows Boaz acting as kinsman redeemer, foreshadowing this pattern.
basic.oneStory: God built repeated, structural release into Israel's calendar so that no family's poverty or bondage would ever be treated as final. That pattern of release finds its true and lasting fulfillment when Christ announces, and accomplishes, true liberty for captives to sin, redeeming His people not for fifty years but forever.

context.literary: This chapter closes the Holiness Code's main run of case law with its most sweeping vision yet, extending the Sabbath principle from a single day to entire years, and structuring Israel's whole economic life around it.
context.historical: Israel's tribal land allotments, assigned by God at the conquest, were meant to be permanent family inheritances; the Jubilee protected this design against the ordinary economic pressures that would otherwise concentrate land and power in fewer hands over generations.
context.canonical: The Jubilee's language of liberty and redemption becomes the explicit text Isaiah later uses for a coming messianic announcement, which Jesus Himself reads aloud and applies directly to His own ministry in Luke 4.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To build structural release, of land, debt, and enslaved persons, into Israel's very calendar, preventing permanent multigenerational poverty and slavery, and grounding the whole system in God's ownership of the land and His prior redemption of Israel from Egypt.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: This chapter prescribes specific old covenant economic law tied to Israel's unique tribal land inheritance; it is not a directly transferable blueprint for modern economic or land policy. Its underlying vision, that poverty and bondage are not to be permanent, is completely fulfilled in Christ's proclamation of true liberty, and continues to inform, though not dictate specific policy for, Christian generosity and justice.

debates.question: Does the Jubilee's economic vision, debt release, return of land, freed slaves, provide a direct blueprint for Christian economic or social policy today?

views.label: A specific old covenant provision tied to Israel's tribal land allotment
views.case: The Jubilee assumes Israel's unique tribal land inheritance system directly given by God at the conquest; it is not designed as a transferable economic system for other nations or the church, which has no equivalent tribal land allotment.

views.label: A revelation of God's heart for periodic release that should shape Christian generosity and justice
views.case: Even without a literal fiftieth year, many read the Jubilee as revealing enduring divine concern for limiting generational poverty, debt bondage, and land concentration, which should inform, though not dictate specific policy for, Christian practices of generosity, debt forgiveness, and justice.

debates.bearing: Both views agree the Jubilee is not directly legislated for the church or civil governments today as a specific fifty year cycle; they differ on how much the underlying concern for economic restoration should shape Christian ethics and advocacy, versus being read primarily as a picture fulfilled spiritually in Christ.

study.originalLanguages: Deror, liberty, is precisely the word Isaiah later uses for a coming proclamation of release, Isaiah 61:1, the very text Jesus reads and claims as fulfilled in Himself in Luke 4. Yovel, Jubilee, likely takes its name from the ram's horn sounded to announce it. Ge'ullah, redemption, names the kinsman's right and duty to buy back a relative's lost land or freedom, grounded entirely in verse 23's declaration that the aretz, land, belongs to God alone.

typology.type: The fiftieth year Jubilee, proclaiming liberty and returning every man to his property
typology.fulfillment: Jesus stands in the Nazareth synagogue and reads Isaiah's Jubilee language, declaring that Scripture proclaiming liberty to the captives is fulfilled that day in Him, Luke 4:18-21.

typology.type: The kinsman redeemer who buys back a relative's lost land or freedom
typology.fulfillment: Christ is the true kinsman redeemer, who buys back His people from slavery to sin, becoming one of us so that He could redeem us, Ruth 4, Galatians 4:4-5.

typology.type: The Sabbath year rest for the land
typology.fulfillment: Anticipates the ultimate rest God's people enter through Christ, ceasing from their own works as God did from His, Hebrews 4:9-10.

study.christ: Luke 4:18-21 records Jesus standing in the Nazareth synagogue and reading Isaiah's words, the Spirit of the Lord is upon Me, because He has anointed Me to proclaim good news to the poor, to proclaim liberty to the captives, deliberately echoing this chapter's Jubilee vocabulary. He then rolls up the scroll and declares, today this Scripture is fulfilled in your hearing, claiming the ancient promise of Jubilee release as accomplished in His own person and mission, not merely a future economic reset but a present spiritual liberation from sin's slavery.
study.god: God declares outright that the land is Mine, relativizing every human claim to permanent wealth, power, or ownership, and reminding Israel they are tenants, not ultimate possessors, of what He gives. He builds repeated, structural points of release into their economic life, the Sabbath year, the Jubilee, so that poverty, debt, and slavery are never treated as final or irreversible, reflecting His own unchanging character as the Redeemer who brought them out of Egypt.

crossReferences.note: Jesus reads Isaiah's Jubilee language in the Nazareth synagogue and declares it fulfilled in Himself that day.
Supplies the exact source text of liberty and release Jesus reads and claims as His own mission.
God sent His Son, born under the law, to redeem those under the law, so that they might receive adoption as sons.
Boaz acts as kinsman redeemer for Naomi and Ruth, embodying the pattern this chapter establishes.

study.oneStory: God built repeated, structural release into Israel's calendar, land resting every seventh year, people and property released every fiftieth, so that no family's poverty or bondage would ever be treated as final. That pattern of release finds its true and lasting fulfillment when Christ stands up and announces, and then accomplishes at the cross, true and permanent liberty for captives to sin, redeeming His people not for fifty years but forever.

quiz.q: What were Israelites forbidden to do with their land during the Sabbath year, according to verses 4-5?

options.text: Sow their fields, prune their vineyards, or reap the aftergrowth of their harvest.
options.note: Verses 4-5 specify all three of these prohibited activities.

options.text: Live in the land at all during that year.
options.note: The land was to rest from cultivation, but the text does not require Israel to leave the land entirely.

options.text: Eat anything the land produced on its own.
options.note: Verses 6-7 explicitly permit eating whatever the land yields naturally during that year.

options.text: No restrictions applied during the Sabbath year.
options.note: Verses 4-5 explicitly restrict sowing, pruning, and formal reaping.

quiz.q: How was the Year of Jubilee announced, according to verse 9?

options.text: By sounding the horn throughout the land on the Day of Atonement.
options.note: Verse 9 specifies this exact timing and method.

options.text: By a public reading of the entire book of Leviticus.
options.note: Verse 9 specifies a trumpet sounded on the Day of Atonement, not a public reading.

options.text: By each family independently deciding when to begin the Jubilee.
options.note: Verse 9 describes a single, unified announcement across the whole land.

options.text: No specific announcement method is given.
options.note: Verse 9 explicitly describes the horn sounded on the Day of Atonement.

quiz.q: What two things happened during the Year of Jubilee, according to verses 10 and 13?

options.text: Liberty was proclaimed, and each person returned to his own property.
options.note: Verses 10 and 13 state both of these outcomes directly.

options.text: All Israelite livestock was sacrificed as a burnt offering.
options.note: The Jubilee concerns land, liberty, and family property, not a mass sacrifice of livestock.

options.text: Israel's borders with neighboring nations were redrawn.
options.note: The Jubilee concerned internal Israelite property and liberty, not international borders.

options.text: Nothing distinctive happened in this particular year.
options.note: Verses 10 and 13 describe distinctive events for this year.

quiz.q: According to verse 23, why could land not be sold permanently in Israel?

options.text: Because the land belongs to the LORD, and Israel are but foreigners and residents with Him.
options.note: Verse 23 states this reasoning explicitly.

options.text: Because Israel had no legal system for property transactions.
options.note: The chapter describes a detailed system for property transactions; the restriction is theological, tied to God's ownership of the land.

options.text: Because all land in Canaan was considered worthless.
options.note: The text describes the land as valuable and worth redeeming, not worthless.

options.text: No reason is given for this restriction.
options.note: Verse 23 explicitly gives the reason.

quiz.q: How does Jesus use this chapter's Jubilee language in Luke 4:18-21?

options.text: He reads Isaiah's echo of it in the Nazareth synagogue and declares it fulfilled in Himself that day.
options.note: Luke 4:18-21 records this event directly.

options.text: He rejects the Jubilee concept as no longer relevant.
options.note: Jesus explicitly embraces and claims fulfillment of this language, rather than rejecting it.

options.text: He never references the Old Testament in His ministry.
options.note: Luke 4:18-21 shows Jesus directly quoting and applying Old Testament Scripture to Himself.

options.text: This chapter has no connection to anything Jesus said or did.
options.note: Luke 4:18-21 draws directly on this chapter's Jubilee vocabulary through Isaiah 61.

questions: God built repeated points of release into Israel's calendar so poverty and debt would never become permanent. Where in your own life or community do you see cycles of hardship being treated as unchangeable, and how might you become part of breaking that cycle?

The Jubilee reminded Israel that the land, and by extension everything they had, ultimately belonged to God, not to them permanently. What possession, position, or achievement do you treat as more permanently and ultimately yours than Scripture would say it actually is?

Jesus stood up and declared the Jubilee's ancient promise of liberty fulfilled in Himself. What captivity in your own life, to fear, shame, addiction, or old patterns of sin, do you need to actually bring to Him for the liberty He offers?

The kinsman redeemer stepped in personally and paid a real cost to restore a relative's lost land or freedom. Who in your life might God be calling you to sacrificially help restore, even at real cost to yourself?

Israel was told never to rule harshly over a fellow Israelite who had fallen into servitude, because they themselves had once been slaves in Egypt. How does remembering your own rescue by grace shape the way you treat those who are currently struggling or dependent on you?

translationNotes.note: The Hebrew word for Jubilee, yovel, is traditionally connected to the ram's horn sounded to announce it, though some scholars debate whether the word instead derives from a root meaning to lead or bring back, fitting the year's function of bringing people back to their property.
Proclaim liberty translates deror, the same word Isaiah later uses, to proclaim liberty to the captives, Isaiah 61:1, a text Jesus reads aloud and applies to Himself in Luke 4:18-19.

The declaration the land is Mine uses a strong possessive construction in Hebrew, functioning as the theological foundation for the entire chapter's laws restricting permanent land sale and requiring its periodic return.
